hook.text: The U.S. federal minimum wage has been $7.25 since 2009—while the cost of living has risen by over 40%.

buildup.text: Minimum wage sets the legal floor for hourly pay—intended to ensure a basic standard of living for workers.

discovery.text: Advocates argue it lifts millions out of poverty. Critics argue it increases unemployment—especially for small businesses.

twist.text: Research is mixed—some studies show minimal job loss from moderate increases, challenging decades of economic orthodoxy.

climax.text: Many cities and states have set their own minimums above the federal level—creating a patchwork of local standards.

punchline.text: The floor hasn't moved in 15 years. The cost of living never stopped.

quiz.question: Since when has the U.S. federal minimum wage remained at $7.25?
